g.data-narration: Primitive types are the fundamental building blocks in Java, storing simple values directly in stack memory for high performance.
g.text: The 8 fundamental data types in Java that represent simple values,
not objects. Stored directly in stack memory for performance.

g.data-narration: Unlike Python where everything is an object, Java keeps primitives separate for speed. They form the foundation of all data manipulation.
g.text: • Form building blocks for all data manipulation in Java
• Direct stack storage = faster than object heap allocation
• Bridge gap with wrapper classes when object representation needed

g.data-narration: Java provides four integral types: byte (8-bit), short (16-bit), int (32-bit), and long (64-bit) for different memory and range needs.
Float (32-bit) and double (64-bit) store decimal values. Double offers greater precision and is the default for decimal literals in Java.
Char is a 16-bit unsigned type holding Unicode characters (0 to 65,535). Boolean represents true/false values for logical operations.
g.text: true or false only | Not an integer

g.data-narration: Instance variables get default values (0, 0.0, false, u0000), but local variables must be initialized before use - a key Java rule.
Widening conversion happens automatically when assigning smaller types to larger types. No data loss occurs, hence no cast needed.
g.text: Automatic conversion from smaller to larger type - no data loss
int num = 100; long l = num; OK double d = num; OK

g.data-narration: Narrowing conversion requires explicit casting because data loss may occur. Use (type) syntax to convert larger to smaller types.
g.text: Requires explicit CAST - potential data loss

g.data-narration: In arithmetic expressions, Java automatically promotes smaller types to larger ones before computation - called binary numeric promotion.
g.text: Binary numeric promotion: smaller types auto-promote to larger before arithmetic
int a = 5; double b = 2.0; a + b promoted to double

g.data-narration: Key rules: float literals need f suffix, long literals need L suffix, underscores improve readability in numbers.
Watch out for: forgetting f suffix, skipping cast in narrowing, treating boolean as integer, and integer overflow silently wrapping.
g.text: float f = 3.14; ERROR: needs f suffix
int x = 10; boolean b = x; ERROR: boolean is not int
byte b = 200; ERROR: overflow for byte range

g.data-narration: Remember the eight primitive types: byte, short, int, long, float, double, char, and boolean. Master the widening hierarchy.
Key takeaways: int is default integer, double is default decimal. Widening is automatic; narrowing needs casting. Default values matter for instance vars.
g.text: int = default int | double = default decimal | Widening = auto | Narrowing = cast | Defaults: 0, 0.0, false, u0000
Practice type conversions and remember: char is UNICODE (not ASCII only)
